stem: [V3-MAPPED-2026] The nurse is reviewing the electronic health record for a 22-year-old client admitted for Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA). The client's initial labs reveal a critical electrolyte imbalance that requires immediate intervention. Based on the provided data, complete the bowtie to address the client's needs.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
S: This is the nurse for the 22-year-old with Type 1 Diabetes in room 3. He was admitted at 07:00 for DKA.
B: He has been non-adherent with his insulin. Initial labs show a glucose of 542 mg/dL, pH of 7.12, and large urine ketones.
A: At 07:15, he has Kussmaul respirations, is tachycardic to 118, and hypotensive at 102/64. His serum potassium is critically low at 3.1 mEq/L.
R: I recommend initiating potassium replacement immediately and holding all insulin until his potassium is above 3.3 mEq/L. Continuous cardiac monitoring is essential.

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content:
TestResultReference Range
Chemistry
Serum Potassium (K+)3.1 mEq/L3.5-5.0 mEq/L
Blood Glucose542 mg/dL70-110 mg/dL
Anion Gap22 mEq/L8-16 mEq/L
Urine KetonesLargeNegative
Arterial Blood Gas
pH7.127.35-7.45
pCO228 mmHg35-45 mmHg
HCO3-10 mEq/L22-26 mEq/L

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
General: Appears weak and lethargic.
Respiratory: Kussmaul respirations, deep and rapid. Lung sounds clear bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Peripheral pulses +2 bilaterally.
Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Complains of headache.
Integumentary: Skin warm and dry, poor skin turgor.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Chest X-Ray: No acute cardiopulmonary abnormalities identified. Lungs are clear. Heart size within normal limits.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content:
Priority Nursing Diagnoses:
- Fluid Volume Deficit related to osmotic diuresis as evidenced by hypotension and poor skin turgor.
- Electrolyte Imbalance (Hypokalemia) related to DKA as evidenced by serum potassium of 3.1 mEq/L.
- Impaired Gas Exchange related to metabolic acidosis as evidenced by Kussmaul respirations and abnormal ABGs.

Nursing Interventions:
- Administer IV fluids as prescribed to restore fluid volume.
- Administer IV potassium chloride as prescribed, monitoring serum potassium levels and EKG continuously.
- Monitor vital signs, respiratory status, and neurological status frequently.
- Provide client education on insulin administration, sick day management, and prevention of DKA.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content:
07:30:
- Potassium Chloride 40 mEq IV in 100 mL Normal Saline over 4 hours (per protocol).
07:45:
- Normal Saline 0.9% 1000 mL IV bolus (prescribed).

conditionOptions.text: DKA with Critical Hypokalemia Shift Risk

actionOptions.text: Hold insulin until potassium is > 3.3 mEq/L
Instruct client to perform slow, deep breathing

hoverRationales.c1: Incorrect. The client has metabolic acidosis (pH 7.12, high anion gap) and critical hypokalemia (K+ 3.1 mEq/L), indicating a complex and unstable condition, not stable hyperglycemia with normal electrolytes.
hoverRationales.c2: Correct. The combination of hyperglycemia, ketosis, metabolic acidosis, and a serum potassium of 3.1 mEq/L confirms DKA. The low potassium is critical because insulin therapy will drive potassium into cells, worsening hypokalemia.
hoverRationales.c3: Incorrect. While DKA can be triggered by infection, the client's temperature is normal and there are no other specific signs of sepsis presented. The clinical picture is classic for DKA.
hoverRationales.a1: Correct. Insulin drives potassium into cells. Administering insulin with a K+ of 3.1 mEq/L would cause life-threatening hypokalemia. The priority is to correct the potassium level before starting insulin.
hoverRationales.a2: Correct. The client's total body potassium is depleted, and the serum level is critically low. IV potassium replacement is essential to prevent cardiac dysrhythmias, especially before insulin therapy is initiated.
hoverRationales.a3: Incorrect. An insulin bolus would rapidly shift potassium into the cells, severely worsening the existing hypokalemia and risking cardiac arrest. This action is contraindicated when K+ is < 3.3 mEq/L.
hoverRationales.a4: Incorrect. The client's Kussmaul breathing is a compensatory mechanism to correct metabolic acidosis by expelling CO2. Instructing slow breathing would counteract this vital compensation and worsen the acidosis.
hoverRationales.p1: Correct. Monitoring potassium is critical due to the initial hypokalemia and the shifts that will occur with treatment. EKG monitoring is essential to detect cardiac effects of hypokalemia, such as U-waves.
hoverRationales.p2: Correct. These parameters are key indicators for resolving DKA. Blood glucose monitoring tracks the response to insulin, while anion gap closure signifies the resolution of the metabolic acidosis.
hoverRationales.p3: Incorrect. Serum albumin is not a priority parameter for monitoring the acute phase of DKA. It is more relevant for assessing long-term nutritional status or conditions like nephrotic syndrome.
hoverRationales.p4: Incorrect. A positive Babinski sign is a neurological finding indicating an upper motor neuron lesion. It is not a relevant parameter for monitoring DKA or its associated electrolyte imbalances.

pearls.title: DKA and Potassium
pearls.description: Insulin administration in DKA drives potassium intracellularly, potentially causing severe hypokalemia. Always check and correct potassium levels before initiating insulin therapy.
pearls.strategy: Monitor potassium levels closely during DKA treatment. Replace potassium as needed to maintain levels above 3.3 mEq/L before and during insulin infusion.

pearls.title: Importance of Anion Gap
pearls.description: The anion gap helps assess the severity of metabolic acidosis in DKA. Its closure indicates resolution of ketoacidosis.
pearls.strategy: Track the anion gap during DKA treatment. A decreasing anion gap suggests effective treatment and resolution of metabolic acidosis.

traps.title: Giving Insulin with Low Potassium
traps.description: Administering insulin without addressing hypokalemia can lead to life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias and respiratory muscle weakness.
traps.strategy: Always review the patient's potassium level before administering insulin. If potassium is low (<3.3 mEq/L), hold insulin and administer potassium replacement as prescribed.

traps.title: Over-Rapid Potassium Correction
traps.description: Rapid potassium infusion can cause cardiac toxicity. High concentrations can cause pain and phlebitis.
traps.strategy: Administer potassium at the prescribed rate and concentration. Monitor the patient for signs of hyperkalemia (e.g., peaked T waves on EKG) and infusion site reactions.

mnemonics.title: DKA Management
mnemonics.description: IV Fluids, Vitals Monitoring, Frequent Glucose Checks, Insulin Administration (after K+ correction), K+ Repletion

mnemonics.title: Hypokalemia Signs
mnemonics.description: Alkalosis, shallow respirations, ST depression, Inverted T waves, Cramps, Weakness, Arrhythmias, Lethargy, Thready pulse
